title: Epic: Core Categorization Engine
description:
Design and implement the core expense categorization engine. This is the central component that takes a submitted expense (amount, description, receipt data) and outputs a category classification + policy match result.

## Open Questions
- Rules-based vs. ML-based approach? Need team input.
- How do we handle ambiguous categories (e.g., monitor arm — is that equipment or furniture)?
- What's the fallback when confidence is low?

title: Epic: Client Policy Configuration & Ingestion
description:
Build the system for ingesting and managing per-client expense policies. Each client has different rules for:
- Allowed expense categories
- Per-category spending limits
- Excluded categories
- Special approval workflows

Need to support both bulk import (from existing client contracts) and a configuration UI for ops team to maintain.

## Requirements
- Schema for policy rules (categories, limits, exclusions)
- Import pipeline for existing client policies
- Validation layer to catch malformed configs
- API for rules engine to query active policies

title: Epic: Admin Dashboard & Manual Review Queue
description:
Build an internal-facing dashboard for AMs and ops to:
- View auto-categorized expenses and their status (approved/denied/flagged)
- Override auto-decisions with manual approve/deny
- See audit trail of all categorization decisions
- Filter by client, category, status, date range

This is critical for the pilot phase — we need humans in the loop until we trust the engine.

title: Epic: Integration Testing & Edge Case Coverage
description:
End-to-end testing framework for the expense auto-categorization pipeline.

- Unit tests for individual categorization rules
- Integration tests for the full submit → categorize → decide pipeline
- Edge case test suite (boundary amounts, ambiguous categories, missing data)
- Load testing for batch expense processing
- Policy config validation tests

title: Implement CategoryMatcher module (rules-based, keyword scoring)
description:
Build the CategoryMatcher component of the auto-categorization pipeline.

## Requirements
- Keyword-based classification with weighted scoring per category
- Categories: `equipment`, `furniture`, `internet`, `ergonomic`, `coworking`, `office_supplies`, `travel`, `other`
- Multi-word phrase matching takes priority over single-keyword matching
- Configurable confidence threshold (default 0.7) — below threshold → `other` + flag for review
- Abstract interface (`ICategoryMatcher`) so we can swap in ML classifier later (v2)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Given a receipt description and amount, returns category + confidence score
- [ ] Multi-word phrases scored higher than single keywords
- [ ] Fallback to `other` when no category exceeds confidence threshold
- [ ] Interface is abstract and implementation-agnostic
- [ ] Unit tests for all standard categories + ambiguous cases

Parent: ENG-201

title: Implement PolicyValidator — per-client policy rule evaluation
description:
Build the PolicyValidator that checks categorized expenses against per-client policy configs.

## Requirements
- Load client policy config (JSON) by client_id
- Validate: (a) category is in allowed list, (b) amount ≤ category limit, (c) category not in exclusions
- Return structured result: `{ allowed: bool, reason: string, rule_matched: string }`
- Handle monthly recurring expenses (e.g., internet) vs one-time

## Policy Schema
```json
{
  \"client_id\": \"string\",
  \"categories\": { \"<category>\": { \"max_amount\": number, \"requires_receipt\": bool } },
  \"excluded_categories\": [\"string\"],
  \"default_action\": \"flag_for_review\" | \"deny\"
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Correctly evaluates policy rules for all category types
- [ ] Returns clear denial reason when policy is violated
- [ ] Handles missing categories gracefully (flags for review)
- [ ] Handles excluded categories (auto-deny with reason)
- [ ] Unit tests for each validation path

Parent: ENG-201", variation_level: moderate

title: Implement DecisionEngine — auto-approve/deny/flag logic
description:
Build the DecisionEngine that takes CategoryMatcher + PolicyValidator outputs and produces a final decision.

## Decision Logic
- `auto_approve`: category confidence ≥ threshold AND policy check passed AND receipt present
- `auto_deny`: category confidence ≥ threshold AND (exceeds limit OR excluded category)
- `flag_for_review`: category confidence < threshold, missing receipt, or amount within 10% of threshold

## Requirements
- Full decision logging with reasoning chain (category scores, policy rule matched, decision rationale)
- Audit trail entry for every decision
- Support manual override flag (for dashboard integration)

Parent: ENG-201

title: Build rule configuration UI for per-client policy management
description:
Create a form-based UI for the ops team to manage per-client expense policy configurations without editing raw JSON.

## Features
- List view of all client policies
- Edit form: add/remove categories, set per-category limits, toggle exclusions
- Preview of generated JSON schema before saving
- Validation feedback (e.g., 'limit must be > 0', 'at least one category required')
- Version history of policy changes

## Notes
- Built as part of the admin dashboard (standalone internal tool)
- Should use the same JSON schema as PolicyValidator
- Chloe (ops) is the primary user — keep it simple

Parent: ENG-202

title: [P1] Fix Mosaic Robotics policy config — malformed JSON types causing skipped exclusion checks and amount validation
description:
## Summary
Mosaic Robotics policy config has malformed JSON field types, causing the PolicyValidator to skip exclusion checks and amount comparisons. Results in out-of-policy expenses being auto-approved.

## Root Cause
Two type errors in the generated Mosaic policy config:

### Issue A: `excluded_categories` is a string instead of an array
```json
// Production (malformed)
\"excluded_categories\": \"coworking\"

// Correct
\"excluded_categories\": [\"coworking\"]
```
The PolicyValidator does an `Array.isArray()` check before evaluating exclusions. Since the field is a string, the check fails and the exclusion logic is skipped entirely. The default path is `approve`.

### Issue B: `max_amount` is a string instead of a number
```json
// Production (malformed)
\"ergonomic\": { \"max_amount\": \"800\", \"requires_receipt\": true }

// Correct
\"ergonomic\": { \"max_amount\": 800, \"requires_receipt\": true }
```
The comparison `1200 <= \"800\"` uses JavaScript lexicographic string comparison, where `\"1\" < \"8\"` is true. So the $1,200 expense passes the $800 limit check.

## Impact
- **2 confirmed false approvals** (Andre Washington: $1,200 standing desk, $350/mo coworking membership)
- **Total financial exposure: $1,550+** (coworking may recur monthly)
- Potentially more false approvals if other Mosaic employees submitted out-of-policy expenses
- Mosaic HR (Priya Delgado) not yet aware — quarterly reconciliation in ~6 weeks

## Reproduction Steps
### Issue A (exclusion skip):
1. Load the production Mosaic config
2. Submit a test expense with category `coworking`, any amount
3. Observe PolicyValidator skips exclusion check (Array.isArray returns false for string)
4. Expense is auto-approved despite coworking being excluded

### Issue B (string comparison):
1. Load the production Mosaic config
2. Submit a test expense with category `ergonomic`, amount $1,200
3. Observe `1200 <= \"800\"` evaluates to true (lexicographic)
4. Expense is auto-approved despite exceeding $800 limit

## Fix Required
1. **Immediate:** Fix Mosaic config — correct types for `excluded_categories` (array) and all `max_amount` fields (number)
2. **Pipeline fix:** Add strict type enforcement in ingestion pipeline output — numbers must be numbers, arrays must be arrays
3. **PolicyValidator hardening:** Add runtime type checks before comparisons — `typeof max_amount !== 'number'` should throw, not silently pass
4. **Validation:** Schema validation on config load that rejects malformed types

## Severity
This is more severe than the Vectral bug (ENG-214). False approvals have direct financial impact — money was paid out that shouldn't have been. False rejections are frustrating but recoverable.

## Related
- ENG-209 (ingestion pipeline — source of type errors)
- ENG-212 (edge case tests — would have caught malformed JSON)
- ENG-214 (Vectral config bug — same pipeline, different failure mode)
- ENG-211 (pilot monitoring)", variation_level: moderate

title: Client policy ingestion pipeline — import existing client policies to JSON configs
description:
Build a pipeline to import existing client policy data into the structured JSON format used by PolicyValidator.

## Requirements
- Parse existing policy docs/spreadsheets from ops into structured JSON
- Generate per-client policy config files
- Validate generated configs against schema
- Handle edge cases: missing fields, ambiguous categories, default values

## Pilot Clients
- Vectral Systems
- Mosaic Robotics
(Will add more clients as we scale)

## Open Questions
- Do we have machine-readable policy docs or will we need manual transcription?
- Waiting on Chloe's requirements doc for actual policy details

Parent: ENG-202

title: [v2] ML-based CategoryMatcher — shadow mode experiment
description:
Explore ML-based classification as an alternative to the rules-based CategoryMatcher.

## Context
Dmitri ran preliminary experiments with TF-IDF + logistic regression on ~250 historical expenses and got 87% category accuracy. The team decided to go rules-based for v1 due to timeline constraints and auditability requirements, but the abstract ICategoryMatcher interface allows for a plug-in ML implementation.

## Proposed Approach
- Train a lightweight classifier (logistic regression or naive Bayes) on historical expense data
- Feature engineering: receipt description tokens, amount ranges, vendor names
- Run in shadow mode alongside rules engine during pilot
- Compare accuracy on real categorizations
- If ML outperforms rules on edge cases, propose as v2 primary classifier

## Requirements for Activation
- Sufficient labeled data (target: 500+ expenses)
- Per-category accuracy ≥ 90%
- Explainability layer (feature importance for each classification)

Deprioritized for now — revisit after pilot results.", variation_level: moderate

title: Pilot Monitoring — Vectral Systems & Mosaic Robotics
description:
Track pilot performance and catch issues during the live prototype phase.

## Pilot Clients
- **Vectral Systems** (AM: Mina Hashimoto)
- **Mosaic Robotics** (AM: Emeka Diallo)

## Monitoring Checklist
- [ ] Daily review of auto-approve/deny/flag decisions for accuracy
- [ ] AMs spot-checking every decision for first week
- [ ] Track categorization accuracy (target: 95%+)
- [ ] Track false positive rate (incorrect auto-approvals)
- [ ] Track false negative rate (incorrect auto-denials)
- [ ] Log any client complaints or escalations
- [ ] Monitor decision latency (target: <500ms per expense)
- [ ] Weekly summary to leadership

## Escalation Path
AMs → #engineering → Samira Tariq
Client HR contacts → AM → #engineering

## Duration
Pilot runs for 2-3 weeks. If accuracy is solid, expand to additional clients.

## Known Risks
- Edge case test coverage still thin (see ENG-204, ENG-212)
- Receipt description quality varies — some vendors use abbreviated/coded descriptions
- AMs may not spot-check every decision after initial novelty wears off

title: Remaining edge case coverage — policy config validation & malformed data handling
description:
Outstanding edge case tests that weren't completed before pilot launch. Flagged by Dmitri Volkov during dev progress update.

## Priority Test Cases
- [ ] Malformed policy config JSON — missing fields, wrong types, null values in nested objects
- [ ] Exclusion list with unexpected data type (string instead of array, empty array, array with duplicates)
- [ ] Generated policy configs match source data from ops spreadsheet (validation tests)
- [ ] Category overlap: comprehensive ergonomic vs. furniture classification tests beyond standard keywords
- [ ] Bundled item receipt descriptions with ambiguous wording
- [ ] Receipt descriptions with vendor abbreviations (e.g., 'IKEA BEKANT DSK')

## Nice-to-Have (post-pilot)
- [ ] Load testing for batch expense processing
- [ ] Stale config detection (warn when `last_updated` > 90 days)
- [ ] Fuzz testing on expense description input

## Context
The engine is live in prototype with real client data. These tests should be completed ASAP to increase confidence in auto-decisions.

Parent: ENG-204

title: Dashboard: Bulk export & policy version history
description:
Non-blocking items from dashboard launch:

1. **Bulk export** — Export expense decisions as CSV for reporting. Finance team will need this for reconciliation.
2. **Policy version history** — Full UI for viewing and comparing policy config changes over time. Stub is implemented but not functional.

De-prioritized for pilot but will be needed before wider rollout.

title: Stipend duplicate detection — receipt hash fingerprinting & PDF metadata similarity scoring
description:
## Background — Audit Findings

In the course of the data quality audit following the pause of ExpenseBot (ENG-216), I identified three questionable stipend submissions that our current processing system handled without conducting any cross-reference checks. The submissions exhibit forensic fingerprints that should have been detected automatically.

### Flagged Transactions

| # | Employee | Client | Item | Vendor | Amount | SHA-256 Hash | PDF Template | Status |
|---|----------|--------|------|--------|--------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | Kevin Tran | Vectral Systems | Ergonomic keyboard — TechComfort ErgoKey Pro | TechComfort Supply | $89.00 | `a3f7c8d1...f0a2b4` | TC-RECEIPT-STD-041 | Paid (3/8) |
| 2 | Andre Washington | Mosaic Robotics | Ergonomic keyboard — TechComfort ErgoKey Pro | TechComfort Supply | $89.00 | `a3f7c8d1...f0a2b4` | TC-RECEIPT-STD-041 | On Hold |
| 3 | Jordan Ekwueme | BrightLoop Analytics | Monitor stand — TechComfort StandPro Mini | TechComfort Supply | $45.00 | `7b9e1d3f...7e9b` | TC-RECEIPT-STD-041 | On Hold |

**Finding A:** Records #1 and #2 are identical PDF files (same SHA-256 hash). Two employees from different client companies submitted exactly the same receipt file.

**Finding B:** Record #3 is a different file (different hash, different content) but contains identical PDF `/Creator` metadata (`TechComfort Supply Invoice Generator v2.1`) and `/Template` ID (`TC-RECEIPT-STD-041`). Structural analysis reveals matching font metrics, field positioning coordinates, and layout geometry — indicating the same template source.

**Financial exposure:** A total of $223 ($89 already disbursed, $134 pending in finance).

### The Gap

Our current pipeline processes each submission in isolation:

```
ExpenseSubmission → CategoryMatcher → PolicyValidator → DecisionEngine → Approve/Deny/Flag
```

At no stage does any component compare a new submission against historical records. There are:

1. **No receipt hash deduplication** — identical files are processed undetected
2. **No PDF metadata fingerprinting** — receipts generated from the same template are not linked
3. **No cross-client comparison** — a receipt submitted under Client A is never compared with receipts from Client B

This allows someone to submit the same receipt PDF to multiple client accounts, with each instance being assessed independently. If the amounts comply with each client's policy limits, every instance is automatically approved.

---

## Proposed Solution — DuplicateDetector Pipeline Stage

Introduce a new `DuplicateDetector` stage that operates **before** `CategoryMatcher` in the pipeline:

```
ExpenseSubmission → DuplicateDetector → CategoryMatcher → PolicyValidator → DecisionEngine
```

### Layer 1: Exact Hash Match (SHA-256)

- Upon receipt upload/ingestion, compute the SHA-256 hash of the uploaded file
- Save the hash in the receipt metadata record (this is already computed for S3 storage deduplication — refer to Lena's comment in #engineering — but it's not currently used for validation)
- Before processing, check the hash index for any existing matches
- **If an exact match is found:** immediately flag the submission as `DUPLICATE_EXACT` and direct it to the manual review queue. Do NOT auto-approve.
- Include in the flag: the matching record ID, employee name, client, and submission date

### Layer 2: PDF Metadata Similarity Scoring

- At ingestion, extract PDF metadata fields: `/Creator`, `/Producer`, `/Template` (custom), `/CreationDate`
- Generate a metadata fingerprint vector from these fields
- Compare against stored fingerprints using a weighted similarity score:
  - Exact match on `/Template`: +0.5 (strongest indicator — same receipt generator template)
  - Exact match on `/Creator`: +0.3 (same software/version)
  - Exact match on `/Producer`: +0.1
  - Matching vendor name in submission fields: +0.1
- **Threshold:** If the similarity score is ≥ 0.5, flag as `DUPLICATE_TEMPLATE_MATCH` and route to manual review
- **If the similarity score is ≥ 0.8** (template + creator + vendor match): flag as `DUPLICATE_HIGH_CONFIDENCE`

### Layer 3: Cross-Client Scope

- Both Layer 1 and Layer 2 must function across the **entire** receipt corpus, not limited to specific clients
- The hash index and metadata fingerprint storage must be global
- This is crucial — the exact situation we identified involved receipts from three distinct client companies

### Dashboard Integration

- A new column in the admin review queue: `Duplicate Status` (`None`, `Exact Match`, `Template Match`, `High Confidence`)
- Clicking the flag will display the matched records side-by-side
- Manual override option available: reviewers can mark a flagged submission as `Verified Legitimate` with a note

### Policy Integration (per Elena's directive)

- Following the CEO's directive (2026-03-15): all stipend submissions exceeding $100 require mandatory manual verification regardless of duplicate status
- The `$100_THRESHOLD_MANUAL_REVIEW` rule should be incorporated into the `DecisionEngine` as a policy exception — even if CategoryMatcher, PolicyValidator, AND DuplicateDetector all approve, submissions above $100 should be directed to the manual queue
- This serves as a separate control from duplicate detection but should be included in the same release

---

## Implementation Notes

- Lena confirmed in #engineering that file hashes are already computed at S3 upload — Layer 1 essentially integrates existing data into the validation pipeline
- Layer 2 necessitates a lightweight PDF metadata parser. Python `PyPDF2` or `pikepdf` can extract `/Creator`, `/Producer`, and custom fields. Minimal new dependency required.
- Fingerprint storage: a simple PostgreSQL table with indexed hash and metadata columns. No need for a vector database at this scale.
- Estimated effort: 2-3 days for Layer 1 + Layer 2, plus 1 day for dashboard integration, plus 1 day for implementing the $100 threshold rule

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] SHA-256 hash calculated and stored for each receipt at ingestion
- [ ] Exact hash match prevents auto-approval and directs to manual review
- [ ] PDF metadata extracted and fingerprinted during ingestion
- [ ] Template similarity scoring flags submissions meeting the threshold
- [ ] Cross-client scope: duplicate checks conducted against the global corpus, not limited to specific clients
- [ ] Dashboard displays duplicate status and side-by-side comparisons
- [ ] $100 threshold manual review rule implemented in the DecisionEngine
- [ ] Backfill: run Layer 1 + Layer 2 against all existing Airtable receipt records and flag any additional matches
- [ ] Unit tests for hash matching, metadata extraction, similarity scoring, and threshold routing

## Related

- ENG-216 — Comprehensive audit of all 47 auto-processed pilot expenses (where this issue was discovered)
- ENG-214 — Vectral configuration fix
- ENG-215 — Mosaic configuration fix
- ENG-212 — Pipeline fortification
- CEO directive (2026-03-15) — $100 manual verification threshold

title: [P1] Fix Vectral Systems policy config — default template limits used instead of custom values
description:
## Summary
Vectral Systems policy config has incorrect limits. The ingestion pipeline fell back to default template values after a silent parse error on the source spreadsheet.

## Root Cause
The policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) uses a try/catch when parsing each client's spreadsheet row. The Vectral row had a merged cell in the ergonomic column, causing a parse error. The catch block swallowed the error and used the default template values instead of aborting.

## Impact
- **2 confirmed false rejections** (Danielle Osei $475 ergonomic chair, Kevin Tran $185 internet setup)
- Potentially more false rejections across other categories
- Rachel Whitfield (Vectral HR) aware and unhappy — client trust damaged

## Incorrect vs. Correct Config Values
| Category | Production (wrong) | Correct (Chloe's data) |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment | $400 | $500 |
| Furniture | $300 | $400 |
| Ergonomic | $400 | $600 |
| Internet | $100/mo | $150/mo |
| Office Supplies | $50 | $75 |

## Reproduction Steps
1. Run the ingestion pipeline against Chloe's spreadsheet (`client_expense_policies_2026.xlsx`)
2. Observe that the Vectral row throws a parse error on the merged ergonomic cell
3. Observe that the catch block logs nothing and falls back to default template
4. Compare generated config against source data — limits don't match

## Fix Required
1. **Immediate:** Manually correct Vectral config with correct limits from Chloe's spreadsheet
2. **Pipeline fix:** Remove silent fallback — parse errors should abort the import with a clear error message
3. **Validation:** Add post-ingestion diff step that compares generated config against source data

## Related
- ENG-209 (ingestion pipeline)
- ENG-212 (edge case tests — would have caught this)
- ENG-211 (pilot monitoring)", variation_level: moderate

title: Invoice email template safety checks — prevent attachment mismatches
description:
Follow-up safeguard after the Terraform pricing leak incident.

Problem:
When finance sends invoice emails manually, the current email template flow does not validate that the attached PDF matches the selected QuickBooks invoice, customer, or recipient account. A user can send a valid email with the wrong PDF attached and there is no blocking check or warning.

Requested safeguards:
1. Validate invoice number in the attached PDF against the selected QuickBooks invoice record before send.
2. Warn if customer name in attachment metadata/text does not match email recipient account.
3. Add a send-time confirmation when an email includes multiple invoice PDFs or when attachment name differs from generated invoice export.
4. Log attachment-to-invoice mapping for audit review.

Context:
Raised after the earlier Terraform pricing exposure so we do not leak client-specific pricing or send mismatched billing documents.

Current status as of Apr 6: deprioritized during engineering triage so the team can focus on DOT compliance work connected to Raj Patel relocation requirements. No assignee.

title: [URGENT] Full audit of all 47 auto-processed pilot expenses
description:
## Per CEO Directive (2026-03-17)

Audit every auto-processed expense during the pilot across both Vectral Systems and Mosaic Robotics.

## Scope
- 47 total auto-processed expenses
- Replay each expense against the correct policy data (from Chloe's original spreadsheet)
- Compare system decision vs. correct decision

## Deliverables
- Spreadsheet with columns: expense_id, employee, client, category, amount, system_decision, correct_decision, is_correct, financial_impact
- Summary: total false rejections, total false approvals, net financial exposure
- Due to Marcus (finance) by Wednesday 3/19 EOD
- Summary included in Elena's Friday status update

## Process
1. Export all decision logs from admin dashboard (Anh helping with export)
2. Load correct policy configs from Chloe's source spreadsheet
3. Replay each expense through the corrected rules engine
4. Flag discrepancies

## Related
- ENG-211 (pilot monitoring)
- ENG-214 (Vectral config fix)
- ENG-215 (Mosaic config fix)

title: [P1] Harden policy ingestion pipeline — remove silent fallback, add type enforcement
description:
## Summary
Fix the policy ingestion pipeline so the two root causes identified in the pilot incidents can never recur.

## Changes Required

### 1. Remove silent fallback to default templates
The current pipeline catches parse errors on individual spreadsheet rows and falls back to the default template. This caused Vectral's config to have wrong limits.

**Fix:** Parse errors abort the entire import with a clear error message: `PolicyIngestionError: Failed to parse row for client '{client_id}' — {error_detail}. Import aborted. No configs were updated.`

### 2. Strict type enforcement on JSON output
The spreadsheet parser reads cell values as-is, preserving text formatting. This caused Mosaic's `max_amount` to be strings and `excluded_categories` to be a string.

**Fix:** Add a type coercion + validation step after parsing:
- `max_amount` → `Number()` with NaN check
- `excluded_categories` → must be Array, reject if string/number/null
- `requires_receipt` → must be Boolean
- `client_id` → must be non-empty String

### 3. Schema validation step before config deployment
Add a validation gate between config generation and deployment:
- JSON Schema validation against the policy config schema
- Diff against previous config version — flag unexpected changes
- Log all changes for audit trail
- Validation failure blocks deployment

### 4. PolicyValidator runtime type guards
Add defensive type checks in PolicyValidator itself:
- `typeof max_amount !== 'number'` → throw `ConfigValidationError`
- `!Array.isArray(excluded_categories)` → throw `ConfigValidationError`
- `Number()` coercion with NaN check as last-resort defense

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Parse error on any row aborts import with clear error
- [ ] All output fields have correct types enforced
- [ ] Schema validation gate blocks malformed configs from deploying
- [ ] PolicyValidator throws on unexpected types instead of silently proceeding
- [ ] Config diff logged on every update
- [ ] All existing tests still pass

## Due: Thursday 3/20

## Related
- ENG-209 (original ingestion pipeline)
- ENG-214 (Vectral config fix — immediate correction deployed)
- ENG-215 (Mosaic config fix — immediate correction deployed)

title: Dashboard: Add type validation to policy config editor
description:
Add frontend validation to the admin dashboard's policy config editor to prevent malformed configs from being saved.

## Validation Rules
- `max_amount` fields must be numeric — reject form submission with error message if not
- `excluded_categories` must serialize as an array — handle the single-item multi-select edge case where JS might output a string
- Required fields: `client_id` (non-empty), at least one category with a positive limit
- Display clear inline validation errors next to the offending fields

## Context
This is the third layer of defense alongside Lena's pipeline type enforcement (ENG-217) and Dmitri's schema validation tests (ENG-212). Even if one layer fails, the others should catch malformed data.

## Due: End of this week (3/21)

Parent epic: ENG-203 (Admin Dashboard)

title: Define launch readiness checklist for features with automated financial decisions
description:
## Context
Per Elena's directive and the pilot incident post-mortem: we launched the expense auto-categorizer before validation tests were complete. The testing gaps were flagged before launch but we proceeded under time pressure. This resulted in 4 false rejections and 3 false approvals across both pilot clients, with $1,550+ in financial exposure.

## Requirements
Define a launch readiness checklist that must be satisfied before any feature that makes automated financial decisions can go to production.

Proposed checklist:
- [ ] All validation tests passing (unit, integration, schema validation)
- [ ] Config data verified against authoritative source (ops spreadsheet, client contracts)
- [ ] Edge case test coverage reviewed and signed off by at least 2 engineers
- [ ] AM sign-off that they've reviewed the tool's outputs in staging with real data
- [ ] Ops sign-off on category definitions and policy configs
- [ ] Finance review of financial decision logic (approve/deny thresholds)
- [ ] 48-hour shadow mode (system makes decisions but doesn't execute them; humans review for accuracy)
- [ ] Rollback plan documented

## Deliverable
Include in the Friday 3/21 status update to Elena. Formalize as a team standard.

## Due: Friday 3/21

title: Apartment Search – Lily Marchetti – Chicago (Canopy Health)
description:
Find pet-friendly one-bedroom apartment options for Lily Marchetti relocating from Nashville to Chicago.

**Client:** Canopy Health
**Relocatee:** Lily Marchetti, UX Researcher
**Destination:** Chicago, IL
**Budget:** $1,800–$2,300/month
**Preferred areas:** Logan Square, Wicker Park, Lincoln Park
**Size:** 1BR
**Pets:** 1 indoor-only cat (8 lbs) — pet-friendly REQUIRED
**Move-in target:** April 25–27
**Special notes:** Prefers a walkable neighborhood

title: Vendor invoice reconciliation — Heartland Movers Q1 2026 (HM-2026-Q1-0042)
description:
## Summary

Heartland Movers submitted their Q1 2026 invoice (HM-2026-Q1-0042) billing for 8 moves at $1,600 each ($12,800 total). Cross-referencing against our Airtable Relocations table (tblRelocations01) revealed only **5 of 8 moves are legitimate Heartland charges**, resulting in a **$4,800 overbilling**.

## Disputed Line Items

### 1. Lily Marchetti — Nashville → Chicago (Canopy Health) — $1,600
- **Reason:** Move was CANCELLED. Employee decided not to relocate.
- **Evidence:** Cancellation email sent to Jake Loomis (Heartland) on **Feb 10, 2026**. No acknowledgment received.
- **Airtable:** RELOC-2026-009 — Status: Cancelled

### 2. Danielle Osei — Phoenix → Denver (Vectral Systems) — $1,600
- **Reason:** Move was CANCELLED. Client pulled relocation request (budget reallocation).
- **Evidence:** Cancellation email sent to Jake Loomis on **Feb 13, 2026**. No acknowledgment received.
- **Airtable:** RELOC-2026-010 — Status: Cancelled

### 3. Priya Venkatesh — Houston → Seattle (GreenStack Energy) — $1,600
- **Reason:** Move was completed by **Swift Relocations**, not Heartland. Electronics-specialist crew was required.
- **Airtable:** RELOC-2026-015 — Vendor: Swift Relocations

## Actions Taken
- Payment hold placed on the full $12,800 invoice in QuickBooks (Marcus Thorne)
- Formal dispute email sent to Jake Loomis at Heartland (Marcus Thorne, Mar 25)
- Discrepancy flagged in vendor reconciliation tracker VRECON-2026-Q1-HM (Hana Kim)
- Cancellation emails + Airtable export attached to dispute email as evidence

## Checklist

- [x] Verify all 8 line items against Airtable Relocations table
- [x] Attach cancellation email evidence for Marchetti (Feb 10) and Osei (Feb 13)
- [x] Confirm Swift Relocations handled the Venkatesh move (not Heartland)
- [ ] Track Heartland's response to the formal dispute
- [ ] Receive corrected invoice for $8,000 (5 verified moves)
- [ ] Release payment hold in QuickBooks once corrected invoice is received
- [ ] Close out reconciliation tracker entry VRECON-2026-Q1-HM

## References
- QuickBooks bill: HM-2026-Q1-0042 (flagged DISPUTED)
- Vendor reconciliation: VRECON-2026-Q1-HM
- Airtable records: RELOC-2026-003, 005, 008, 009, 010, 011, 015, 016
- #operations thread with full findings breakdown
- #finance summary from Marcus

title: Apartment Search – Dr. Yusuf Abdi – Chicago (Canopy Health)
description:

title: Rebalance April account coverage for Emeka across Sunbelt, Palmetto, Axiom, and Mosaic
description:
Emeka flagged overload in #operations on 2026-04-05. He is currently covering five active relocations across four clients and is already behind on follow-ups.

Accounts called out:
- Sunbelt Venture Partners
- Palmetto Community Foundation
- Axiom Precision Manufacturing
- Mosaic Robotics April relocation wave

Ask from ops: review whether coordinator coverage can be shifted and whether one account thread can be temporarily reassigned for 1-2 weeks before service quality drops.

Source Slack message: Morning all — quick flag before this turns into a client-service problem. I’m carrying five active relocations across four clients right now and I’m behind on a couple of follow-ups already. Current load is Sunbelt Venture Partners, Palmetto Community Foundation, Axiom Precision Manufacturing, and the Mosaic Robotics April wave. Between client comms, coordinator handoffs, and invoice/admin cleanup, I’m getting a little underwater.

<@moveops_chloe_vance> is there any way we can shift coordinator coverage or move one account thread for the next 1-2 weeks? I can keep things moving, but I’d rather raise it now than wait until something slips.

title: Consolidate Heartland Q1 billing discrepancies into vendor dispute response
description:
Hana widened the Heartland review on April 14 after reconciling all five Q1 Heartland moves against service authorizations, coordinator notes, and invoice backup.

Current findings:
- 3 of 5 Q1 Heartland moves show discrepancies
- Marchetti / Osei remains disputed under VRECON-2026-Q1-HM with $4,800 in invalid billing already documented
- Kevin Tran file includes 2 days of storage billed without any signed contract authorization or ops addendum
- Priya Nair file includes an apparent shuttle fee mismatch versus origin access notes and approved scope

Need a single finance position before responding to Heartland's April 13 formal notice.

Action items:
- Confirm disputed amounts for Kevin Tran and Priya Nair
- Pull supporting documents from operations and prior coordinator notes
- Prepare consolidated dispute packet for Marcus to send to Heartland
- Decide whether to keep payment hold at invoice level or widen hold to related Q1 charges

Context:
- Heartland formal notice received April 13
- Earlier vendor dispute ticket has not moved in two weeks, which is now creating negotiation risk

title: Build consolidated Q3 vendor margin dataset across Airtable, QuickBooks, and CRM
description:
Alejandro started the Q3 cost exposure model after the Heartland and Swift pricing changes and found there is no single source of truth for margin forecasting.

Context:
- Heartland formal Q3 rate adjustment arrived in Formal Notice: Q3 2026 Rate Adjustment and Account Review
- Swift imposed immediate pricing changes and DOT paperwork surcharge in Pricing Update Effective Immediately — DOT Documentation Surcharge
- Hana's dispute work in Consolidate Heartland Q1 billing discrepancies into vendor dispute response shows actual vendor billing is already messy, which weakens any forward-looking margin model if we do not normalize the history first.

Required dataset for finance/executive planning:
- relocation record ID
- client account
- employee transferee name
- assigned vendor
- lane/corridor
- booked vendor estimate
- actual vendor bill amount from QuickBooks
- surcharge flags including DOT documentation fees
- move stage / expected ship date
- projected Q3 move volume from CRM pipeline or account plans

Blockers identified:
- Airtable vendor assignment fields are incomplete or inconsistent across active moves
- QuickBooks bills are not consistently tagged to relocation records or client accounts
- CRM upcoming volume is separate from operations data and difficult to map by vendor exposure

Immediate objective:
Create a working finance dataset good enough to support a client-by-client margin sensitivity model for Q3 and to inform Marcus's vendor strategy response.

Suggested first pass:
1. Pull all active and forecasted Q2/Q3 relocations from Airtable
2. Map historical Heartland and Swift bills from QuickBooks to those records where possible
3. Overlay CRM account pipeline for expected move volume
4. Mark gaps explicitly instead of backfilling unsupported assumptions

title: Follow up with Jae-won Kim on requested Austin lease documents
description: Customer support follow-up from email_email_1a3fb965161a. Omar checked the relocation record before triage; Airtable shows Status Completed and apartment Confirmed, and the main relocation ticket was already closed, so this was categorized as a low-priority document request rather than an active housing exception. title: DOT Docs — Mei-Lin Zhao / GreenStack / Class 9 Lithium
description:
Verify and compile complete DOT hazardous materials documentation for the Mei-Lin Zhao relocation (GreenStack Energy, Houston TX to Portland OR).

Material: Class 9 lithium-ion battery test modules (~350 lbs, UN3481-certified packaging)
Carrier: Swift Relocations (PHMSA Registration #3281074)
Move window: April 17 - April 28, 2026
Airtable record: recMeiLinZhaoHoustonPortland

Documentation checklist:
(A) Shipping papers — VERIFIED. Swift provided completed Class 9 shipping papers with proper shipping name, UN3481 designation, packing group, and quantity description. On file.
(B) Hazmat placarding certificate — VERIFIED. Signed certificate from Swift driver confirming Class 9 placards affixed to transport vehicle per 49 CFR 172.500-560. On file.
(C) Carrier certification — VERIFIED. Swift PHMSA registration #3281074 confirmed valid through 2027. On file.
(D) Emergency response documentation — VERIFIED. Emergency contact sheet provided to Swift dispatch per 49 CFR 172.602. On file.

All four documentation categories complete. Ready for inclusion in DOT response package.

title: DOT Docs — Keiko Tanaka / Mosaic / Class 3B Laser
description:
Verify and compile complete DOT hazardous materials documentation for the Keiko Tanaka relocation (Mosaic Robotics, Columbus OH to Seattle WA).

Material: Epilog Fusion Pro 48 industrial laser cutter (65"x42"x44", 400 lbs, ~$35K value) with Class 3B CO2 laser tube (75W). Per DOT 49 CFR Part 173, laser power supply requires hazmat placarding for transport.
Carrier: Swift Relocations (PHMSA Registration #3281074)
Move window: April 15 - April 23, 2026
Coordinator: Blessing Okafor
Airtable record: recKeikoTanakaColumbusSeattle

Documentation checklist:
(A) Shipping papers — VERIFIED. Swift shipping manifest references laser equipment classification. On file.
(B) Hazmat placarding certificate — MISSING. Airtable record explicitly states DOT placarding required and that Swift must sign a liability waiver acknowledging laser classification before pickup. No signed placarding certificate found in shared drive, Airtable attachments, or email records. Blessing Okafor confirmed she received verbal confirmation from the Swift driver that placards were affixed at time of transport but never followed up to obtain the signed certificate document. The paperwork was missed because she was simultaneously coordinating the Tyler Okonkwo (Canopy Health) and Zara Okoye (Mosaic prototype) moves the same week.
(C) Carrier certification — VERIFIED. Swift PHMSA registration #3281074 confirmed valid. On file.
(D) Emergency response documentation — VERIFIED. Standard emergency response sheet on file with Swift dispatch.

STATUS: CRITICAL GAP. Missing placarding certificate for a DOT-regulated hazmat shipment. Under 49 CFR 107.329, this could be treated as a standalone violation with penalties up to $59,017 per day. Immediate remediation required — contact Swift Relocations to obtain the signed certificate retroactively.

REMEDIATION PLAN: Chloe to contact Greg Pallone at Swift Relocations directly to request the signed placarding cert. If Swift's driver completed the placards as verbally reported, Swift should have the documentation on file per their 3-year DOT retention policy.

title: DOT Docs — Raj Patel / NorthWind / Lithium UPS
description:
Verify and compile complete DOT hazardous materials documentation for the Raj Patel relocation (NorthWind Technologies, Chicago to Denver).

Material: 4 rack-mounted servers with lithium UPS batteries (~2,400 lbs total). Class 9 hazmat handling and DOT-certified transport required.
Carrier: Swift Relocations (PHMSA Registration #3281074)
Move window: April 21 - April 23, 2026
Airtable record: recRajPatelChicagoDenver

Documentation checklist:
(A) Shipping papers — VERIFIED. Class 9 lithium battery shipping papers from Swift with proper UN identification numbers and hazard class designations. On file.
(B) Hazmat placarding certificate — VERIFIED. NorthWind's CISO required an extensive chain-of-custody protocol including supervised loading. The chain-of-custody log includes a signed placarding confirmation as part of the supervised loading documentation. On file.
(C) Carrier certification — VERIFIED. Swift PHMSA registration #3281074 confirmed valid. On file.
(D) Emergency response documentation — VERIFIED. NorthWind IT security team maintained separate emergency contact during transport in addition to Swift's standard emergency response sheet. Both on file.

Note: NorthWind's strict chain-of-custody requirements actually produced the most thorough documentation package of any hazmat move in our history. The CISO-mandated supervised loading protocol with signed handoff at every transfer point means we have redundant verification at every step.

All four documentation categories complete. Ready for inclusion in DOT response package.

title: DOT Docs — Joaquin Reyes / GreenStack / Oversize Rig

This move is NOT hazmat-classified but was flagged in the DOT inquiry because the overweight transport permit references MoveOps as the coordinating party. Including this documentation in our DOT response package for completeness and to demonstrate transparency.

All applicable documentation complete. Ready for inclusion in DOT response package.

title: Equipment Packing & Chain-of-Custody — Dr. Mehta — Medical Research Equipment
description:

Relocatee: Dr. Anand Mehta, Chief Medical Officer (new hire)
Type: Executive relocation — VIP / white-glove

   - Approximate value: $12,000 each ($24,000 total)
   - Requirements: Anti-static packaging, calibration certificates must travel with units
   - Cannot be recalibrated remotely — physical handling must preserve calibration state
2. One (1) SentrySafe DH-109E secured filing cabinet
   - Approximate weight: 350 lbs
   - Contents: Patient research data binders (compliance-sensitive material)
   - Requirements: Formal chain-of-custody documentation per Canopy Health compliance policy
   - Signed handoff log required at both origin (Boston) and destination (Chicago)
   - No gaps in custody chain permitted — direct transport, no interim warehouse storage

Action items:
1. Confirm full equipment inventory with Dr. Mehta (make, model, serial numbers, dimensions)
2. Obtain chain-of-custody documentation template from Canopy Health compliance team (coordinate with Patricia Langford)

4. Coordinate supervised loading at Boston origin — Canopy may require compliance officer present for filing cabinet handoff
5. Prepare signed handoff log for destination delivery in Chicago
6. Ensure calibration certificates are physically packaged with monitors (not shipped separately)

Risk: This is the highest-risk workstream. Any damage to the monitors voids calibration ($12K per unit to replace). Any gap in chain-of-custody for the research data cabinet is a compliance violation for Canopy.

Type: Executive relocation — VIP / white-glove
Account Manager: Mina Hashimoto

Move Details:
- Origin: Boston, MA (exact address TBD — to be confirmed with Dr. Mehta)
- Destination: Chicago, IL (condo address TBD — closing in late May, so delivery goes to temporary corporate housing initially or to condo if timing aligns)
- Target pack/load date: April 30 at latest
- Target delivery: May 2-3 in Chicago
- Must-arrive deadline: May 5 (first day at Canopy Health HQ)
- Vendor: Swift Relocations (preferred — strong track record on Boston corridor and electronics handling)
- Partner: Priya Mehta, 7 months pregnant — absolutely zero physical effort required from her. Full packing service, all boxes, furniture disassembly/reassembly.

Scope:
- Full-service pack and load (white-glove, all rooms)
- Long-distance transport Boston to Chicago (~850 miles, typically 2-3 day transit)
- Unload and unpack at Chicago destination
- Furniture reassembly
- NOTE: Medical research equipment (monitors + filing cabinet) may ship on same truck or separately depending on chain-of-custody requirements — coordinate with Equipment Packing workstream

Action items:
1. Contact Swift Relocations for quote and availability on April 30 pack date
2. Confirm Boston pickup address with Dr. Mehta
3. Determine Chicago delivery address (temp housing or condo — depends on housing workstream timing)
4. Request Swift's electronics-specialist crew for the monitors if shipping together with household goods
5. Confirm full packing service — Dr. Mehta and partner should not pack anything themselves
6. Arrange for building access / elevator reservations at both origin and destination

Dependencies:
- Temporary housing confirmation (determines delivery address if condo not ready)
- Equipment packing workstream (may consolidate onto same truck or require separate transport)

title: BrightLoop account audit: reopen unresolved April relocations before May expansion
description:

- CRM context currently overstates the April BrightLoop batch as completed/smooth.

This needs to be treated as both service recovery and account risk before pricing/scoping the six new Boston relocations.

title: Prepare CEO vendor strategy memo covering Heartland, Swift, and backup capacity options

title: StormCloud account incident reconstruction — Jae-won housing, stipend hold, referral drop
description:
Opened after Liam Park's 4/24 escalation. This is a cross-functional reconstruction of failures affecting StormCloud.

Confirmed chain so far:
1) Carmen Reyes emailed on 2026-04-08 that Jae-won Kim's Austin lease was still pending the corporate guarantor packet and landlord countersignature. See Urgent: Jae-won Kim Austin lease still pending guarantor packet.
2) Suki Patel replied promising follow-up but did not send the documents. See Re: Urgent: Jae-won Kim Austin lease still pending guarantor packet.
3) Relocation issue StormCloud Relocation – Jae-won Kim – SEA → AUS was closed prematurely while Jae-won was still in temporary housing.
4) Airtable relocation record recJaeWonKimStormCloudAustin still presents the move as completed, which fed bad status downstream.
5) Omar Ibrahim deprioritized Jae-won's lease-docs request based on those incorrect fields. Related support issue: Follow up with Jae-won Kim on requested Austin lease documents.
6) Simone Richter's legitimate standing desk reimbursement is still frozen under finance's blanket hold.
7) StormCloud CEO referral for Ridgeline Robotics was celebrated internally but no CRM lead/deal or reply was created; Kenji Watanabe followed up again on 2026-04-21.

Need each function to verify scope, impact, and immediate correction path before Mina responds to client.
